one_line_takeaway: A USENIX-style formal-methods paper that ports cryptographic security-game methodology (algorithms, completeness games, adversary capability sets, advantage/negligibility, composable security) onto LM-based agentic systems, producing an attack taxonomy, a unified confidentiality/integrity/availability security game, a proved composability theorem for conjunctive helpfulness/harmlessness predicates, and a formal re-reading of four existing agentic access-control frameworks (FIDES, Progent, Conseca, Controlvalve) -- with no empirical experiments, datasets, or benchmarks.
key_finding: The paper's central formal claim is that confidentiality of training data (in the DPD/differential-privacy-style sense borrowed from prior SoK work, refs [26,27]) is fundamentally in tension with completeness/utility -- illustrated informally via a cat-vs-dog classifier that, if made DPD-secure against membership inference on any single training element, loses the ability to distinguish cats from dogs. It also formally shows (Theorem 1) that a modular 'dual construction' -- pairing a generative AIOracle (CAIO) with a decisional filtering AIOracle (BAIO) -- preserves completeness and ATK-psi-security under conjunction of predicates, giving a principled way to compose security properties the way cryptographic protocol composition does. Separately, the paper translates a cited impossibility result (Ball, Gluch, Goldwasser, Kreuter, Reingold, Rothblum, arXiv:2507.07341, on the computational intractability of output filtering for LLM alignment) into its own AIOracle/CAIO/BAIO/phi vocabulary, explaining that construction as a CAIO' that embeds a covert channel undetectable by any BAIO -- this specific impossibility result is explicitly attributed to the cited paper, not claimed as this paper's own finding.
core_contribution: The paper (Villa, Durak, Kohno, Maharramli, Roesner) is a theory/formal-methods paper, not an empirical one. Its own contributions are: (1) the 'AIOracle' abstraction -- an algorithmic model of LMs/chatbots/classifiers/agents built around a LEARN phase (itself split into r rounds of (GENERATE_DATA_i, LEARN_i) to let attacks target specific training rounds) and a single INFER phase taking (prompt, context, model); (2) a multi-dimensional attack taxonomy for LMs along four axes -- attack vector (data-based vs prompt-based), attack phase (learning vs inference), adversarial knowledge (white-box/black-box, no grey-box), and adversary source (user/third-party/supply-chain) -- plus an 'attack persistence' axis (transient vs persistent), applied to six attack categories (prompt injection, jailbreak, data exfiltration, membership inference, data poisoning, backdoor) summarized in the paper's own Table 1; (3) a formal 'completeness' game (Definition 2, Algorithm 2/completeness pseudocode) modeling correct/benign-case behavior, and a full adversarial 'Security_Game' (Algorithm 4) parameterized by an attacker capability set ATK = {see_model, inject_learn, inject_infer, black_box} and a triviality predicate psi(trace), unifying confidentiality/integrity/availability into one game with advantage Adv = Pr[Security_Game -> 1] - (1-p) and an ATK-psi-security definition (Definition 3); (4) an argument/claim (stated as proved in an appendix subsection E.1 not present in the first 22 pages extracted here) that DPD-style (differentially-private-distinguishability) confidentiality of training data is fundamentally in tension with completeness/utility, illustrated with a cat-vs-dog classifier example; (5) a 'dual construction' that builds an AIOracle for a conjunctive predicate phi = phi_1 AND phi_2 from a 'creative AIOracle' (CAIO, solves the computational/generative problem) and a 'boring AIOracle' (BAIO, solves the decisional/filtering problem for phi_1'), with Theorem 1 stating the composition is complete and ATK-psi-secure if both components are (proof sketch pointed to Appendix E.2, not in the extracted pages); and (6) a comparative analysis (Table 2, Section 5) mapping four existing agentic access-control mechanisms -- FIDES, Progent, Conseca, and Controlvalve -- onto the paper's own formalism, including an original critique of Progent's LM-assisted dynamic policy update (proposing a Dual-LLM-pattern fix) and of Conseca's 'LM-to-secure-LM paradox' where the planner and policy generator share overlapping trusted context.
threat_model: The paper's own threat model is abstract/algorithmic, not empirical: an adversary A interacts with the AIOracle's LEARN and/or INFER phases according to which flags are set in ATK. see_model grants the adversary a view of the trained model (white-box, e.g. open-weight models such as Llama/DeepSeek per the paper's own example); its absence plus black_box models pure query access (e.g., the paper names Claude as an example of a black-box-only target); inject_learn lets the adversary substitute a poisoned corpus in round i of LEARN; inject_infer lets the adversary choose the context passed to INFER (the vector for prompt injection/instruction hijacking). The adversary's source is classified as end user, third party (poisoning external/tool data), or supply-chain (poisoning training data). The paper explicitly excludes grey-box adversarial knowledge from its games. A triviality-filter predicate psi(trace) is used to rule out degenerate wins (e.g., an adversary that poisons training data to teach 'elephants can fly' and then simply asks that question does not count as breaking security).
limitations: The paper itself acknowledges (Section 5.2, per-framework 'Limitations and trade-offs' subsections) that its formalism is a lens applied post hoc to existing systems (FIDES, Progent, Conseca, Controlvalve) rather than something those systems were designed against, and that the predicate phi (helpfulness/harmlessness) is explicitly stated to be 'not implementable' -- the whole security-game apparatus is built atop an oracle predicate the authors say cannot itself be computed, which is a foundational limitation the paper states outright rather than resolves. No empirical validation of the taxonomy's coverage or the security games against real attacks/systems is presented in the examined pages.

baselines_compared: FIDES (Costa et al., arXiv:2505.23643) -- information-flow-control / lattice-based access control with confidentiality+integrity data labels, analyzed qualitatively (not benchmarked) in Section 5.2.1 and Table 2

Progent (Shi et al., arXiv:2504.11703) -- JSON-schema-based privilege control DSL with LM-assisted policy generation (Progent-LM), analyzed qualitatively in Section 5.2.2 and Table 2; paper's own critique proposes a Dual-LLM-pattern fix to its policy-update vulnerability

Conseca (Tsai & Bagdasarian, HotOS 2025) -- planner/executor split with just-in-time context-aware policies, analyzed qualitatively in Section 5.2.3 and Table 2; paper's own critique identifies an 'LM-to-secure-LM paradox' and vulnerability to adversarial planning attacks
Controlvalve (Jha et al., arXiv:2510.17276) -- control-flow-integrity-based two-stage (CFG check + LM judge) defense, analyzed qualitatively in Section 5.2.4 and Table 2
